questions.questionText: In ATLS, the primary survey follows the sequence:
questions.optionB: A (Airway with C-spine), B (Breathing), C (Circulation), D (Disability/Neuro), E (Exposure/Environment)
questions.explanation: ATLS Primary Survey: systematic ABCDE approach. A: Airway maintenance with C-spine protection (jaw thrust, NOT head tilt in trauma). B: Breathing — look, listen, feel (pneumothorax, hemothorax, flail chest). C: Circulation — hemorrhage control, IV access, fluid resuscitation. D: Disability — GCS, pupils, lateralizing signs. E: Exposure — completely undress, prevent hypothermia. Adjuncts: FAST ultrasound, CXR, pelvic XR. Reference: ATLS 10th Edition.

questions.questionText: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis requiring:
questions.optionB: Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS, MCL) followed by chest tube — do NOT delay for imaging
questions.explanation: Tension pneumothorax: life-threatening emergency — air enters pleural space but cannot exit (one-way valve). Signs: hypotension, tachycardia, distended neck veins (JVP elevated), tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side, absent breath sounds, hyperresonance. Treatment: IMMEDIATE needle decompression (14-16G needle, 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line) → definitive: tube thoracostomy (5th ICS, anterior axillary line). DO NOT wait for CXR. Reference: ATLS — Tension Pneumothorax.

questions.questionText: FAST (Focused Assessment with Sonography for Trauma) examines which areas?
questions.optionB: Pericardial (subxiphoid), right upper quadrant (Morrison's pouch), left upper quadrant (splenorenal), and pelvis (suprapubic/Douglas) — detects free fluid
questions.explanation: FAST: bedside ultrasound in trauma. 4 views: (1) Subxiphoid/pericardial — cardiac tamponade. (2) RUQ/Morrison's pouch (hepatorenal) — most sensitive for free fluid. (3) LUQ/splenorenal — splenic injury. (4) Suprapubic/pelvis — pelvic free fluid. E-FAST (extended): adds bilateral lung views for pneumothorax/hemothorax. Detects free FLUID (not organ injury). Positive FAST in hemodynamically unstable patient = operative intervention. Reference: ATLS — FAST.

questions.questionText: The most commonly injured abdominal organ in blunt trauma is:
questions.optionB: Spleen (most common in blunt abdominal trauma — encapsulated, vascular, fixed by ligaments)
questions.explanation: Blunt abdominal trauma: spleen #1 (most common — friable, vascular, protected only by thin capsule). Liver: #2 in blunt (but #1 in penetrating). Spleen: Kehr's sign (left shoulder pain from diaphragmatic irritation by splenic blood). Management: hemodynamically stable → CT with contrast → conservative (if grade I-III, no active bleed). Unstable → OR (splenectomy/splenorrhaphy). Post-splenectomy: vaccination (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib) + OPSI risk. Reference: Bailey & Love — Splenic Injury.

questions.questionText: Beck's triad of cardiac tamponade includes:
questions.optionB: Hypotension, distended neck veins (elevated JVP), and muffled heart sounds
questions.explanation: Cardiac tamponade (Beck's triad): (1) Hypotension (decreased cardiac output), (2) Distended neck veins/elevated JVP (impaired venous return), (3) Muffled/distant heart sounds. Also: pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in SBP during inspiration). ECG: electrical alternans, low voltage. Diagnosis: FAST (pericardial effusion). Emergency: pericardiocentesis (subxiphoid approach). Definitive: surgical pericardial window/thoracotomy. Reference: ATLS — Cardiac Tamponade.
